title: Node Runtime Core
description: Node runtime and architecture questions focused on event loop, workers, and I/O.

cards.question: Is Node.js single-threaded?
cards.shortAnswer: The JavaScript execution model is centered on a single-threaded event loop, but Node itself is not limited to one thread overall.
cards.expectedAnswer: Node.js runs JavaScript primarily on a single-threaded event loop, but the runtime is not limited to one thread overall. It uses libuv, can offload some work to a thread pool, and can use worker threads for CPU-bound tasks. So the right answer is that the main JS execution path is single-threaded, but the runtime can still use multiple threads.
keyPoints: runtime can use thread pool or workers
distinguish JS execution from the whole runtime
commonTraps: answering simply yes or no without nuance
assuming async automatically means multi-threaded execution
followUps: When would you use worker threads?
What kind of work blocks the event loop?

cards.question: What is the difference between worker threads and cluster in Node.js?
cards.shortAnswer: Worker threads help with CPU-bound work inside one process, while cluster runs multiple Node processes to use multiple cores and isolate failures more cleanly.
cards.expectedAnswer: Worker threads are useful when you need CPU-bound parallelism inside a process and want to offload heavy computation from the main event loop. Cluster is about running multiple Node processes, often to use multiple CPU cores for request handling and get stronger process isolation. They solve related but different problems.
keyPoints: cluster for multiple processes and core usage
different tradeoffs around isolation and communication
followUps: What is the communication cost with workers?
When would horizontal scaling be simpler?
